reasoning: The simulated news indicates that as of April 8-9, a ceasefire is in place but it is "shaky" and shippers are remaining hesitant to cross the Strait of Hormuz, with just a trickle of ships passing through. A Guardian article on April 8 notes that "Ceasefire changes little for shipping... no permission, no transit", while CNN on April 9 states "Just two oil or gas tankers have transited...". US marines are being sent to investigate how to restore traffic. With the present date being April 12 and the resolution date being May 1 (only 18 days left), and current daily figures nowhere near the required 12 per day limit, the probability of hitting 12 tankers in a single day heavily depends on a massive sudden restoration of confidence or a successful US naval escort operation. Given the logistical timelines of assembling naval escorts (requiring 8-10 destroyers per Lloyd's List, capping volumes at <10%), and the general hesitation of shipping companies, it seems unlikely they will scale up to 12 a day within the next two weeks. I am downward-adjusting from the April 2 community forecast of 0.60 to 0.40 in light of the continued paralysis in transit despite the recent ceasefire.
